Iceland's Fishing Heritage at Stake: Why EU Membership Would Be a Disaster

By Jón Gunnarsson | Published 15 February 2026

Iceland's fishing industry is the backbone of our economy, and EU membership would put it all at risk. The Common Fisheries Policy (CFP) would force Iceland to hand over control of its fishing waters to Brussels bureaucrats who have no understanding of North Atlantic marine ecosystems.

Under the CFP, Iceland would lose control of its 200-mile exclusive economic zone. EU fleets from Spain, France, and Portugal would gain access to Icelandic fishing grounds, devastating local communities that depend on fishing for their livelihoods. The EU's track record on fisheries management is catastrophic — the CFP has led to widespread overfishing and a 40% decline in European fishing fleets over the past two decades.

Consider the sovereignty implications. Iceland has managed its fisheries sustainably through the Individual Transferable Quota (ITQ) system since the 1980s. Our fish stocks are among the healthiest in the world precisely because we control our own waters. The EU would replace this proven system with centralised quotas set in Brussels.

Norway provides a perfect example. Despite enormous pressure, Norway has twice voted against EU membership and manages its fisheries independently through the EEA Agreement. Norwegian fish stocks are thriving, and their fishing industry is more profitable than ever. If Norway can succeed outside the EU, so can Iceland.

The economic numbers are stark. Fishing and fish processing account for over 40% of Iceland's export earnings. Under the CFP, Iceland's annual catch allocation could be reduced by as much as 30%, costing the industry billions of krónur. Small fishing villages around Iceland would face economic collapse.

EU proponents argue that membership would open new markets, but Iceland already has tariff-free access to the EU market for most goods through the EEA Agreement. What exactly would we gain that we don't already have?

Furthermore, EU membership would mean accepting the Common Agricultural Policy, which would flood the Icelandic market with subsidised European food products, undermining local farmers. Iceland's agricultural sector, already fragile, simply cannot compete with the scale of continental European farming.

The bottom line is clear: EU membership would sacrifice Iceland's most valuable natural resource — its fish — for nebulous promises of economic integration that we largely already enjoy through the EEA. Our sovereignty over our waters is non-negotiable.
